intent1: Stand up the `flowctl` CLI so a pipeline described in YAML actually runs end

rounds.title: Build the first cut of `flowctl`
rounds.md:
# Build the first cut of `flowctl`

Stand up the `flowctl` CLI so a pipeline described in YAML actually runs end
to end: read a structured file, reshape each record through an ordered list
of stages, and write the surviving records to an output sink. The project
skeleton in `/app/` already has the module path, a README, and a couple of
smoke fixtures — everything else you add.

## Build & invocation

- The binary must be produced by `go build -o /app/flowctl ./cmd/flowctl` from
  inside `/app/`. No other build command is needed.
- The module (`go.mod`) must stay at module path `example.com/flowctl` and
  must not require any third-party dependency — use only the Go standard
  library. The container has no network access at run time.
- Invocation:

  ```
  /app/flowctl run --config <path-to-pipeline.yaml> [--strict]
  ```

  Any other subcommand, or an unknown flag, must fail with a non-zero exit
  code and a single line on stderr starting with `flowctl: `.

## Pipeline config format

`flowctl` reads a YAML pipeline file with three top-level keys: `input`,
`stages`, `output`. All three are required; omitting any of them must fail
with a non-zero exit code and a stderr message starting with `flowctl: config:`.

### `input`

```yaml
input:
  path: orders.csv
  format: csv      # or "ndjson"
```

- `path` is resolved relative to the directory containing the config file.
- `format` must be either `csv` or `ndjson`. Any other value is a config
  error (`flowctl: config: ...`).
- **CSV**: the file has a header row; every subsequent row becomes a record
  whose field names come from the header and whose values are strings. Rows
  with a different column count than the header are a hard error and must
  abort the run with a non-zero exit code.
- **NDJSON**: one JSON object per line. Empty lines (only whitespace) are
  skipped. Every object's keys and values must be strings; a value that is
  not a JSON string (e.g. a number, `null`, array, or nested object) is a
  hard error and must abort the run. Different lines may have different
  key sets; in that case the record's "field order" is the order the keys
  appear in that line's JSON text, and a union header (see `output` below)
  is taken across all input records in first-seen order.

### `stages`

`stages` is an ordered YAML list. Each entry has a `type` and its own config.
An empty list is legal and means "pass records through unchanged". Four
stage types must be supported:

#### `filter`

```yaml
- type: filter
  where:
    column: status
    op: "=="
    value: paid
```

- `column` is the record field to inspect.
- `op` is one of `==`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=`.
- `value` is a scalar (string or number) from the YAML.
- Comparison rules:
  - For `==` and `!=`: compare as strings.
  - For `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=`: both the record field and the value must parse
    as finite decimal numbers (IEEE-754 `float64`, no `NaN` or `Inf`). If
    either side fails to parse, that **record** is dropped; a line
    `flowctl: filter: drop non-numeric row <n>` (1-based input record
    number, counted in the order records were produced by the input source,
    independent of any earlier stage) is written to stderr. The run itself
    continues and does not fail — unless `--strict` is set, in which case
    the run aborts with a non-zero exit code on the first such drop.
- A record that does not satisfy the predicate is removed from the stream.
- A record whose `column` is missing from the current record is also
  removed; the first such occurrence per run emits
  `flowctl: filter: missing column <name>` on stderr. Under `--strict`,
  the first occurrence aborts the run.

#### `project`

```yaml
- type: project
  columns:
    - order_id
    - name: customer
      as: buyer
    - amount
```

- Each list entry is either a bare string (keep the column, same name) or a
  mapping with `name` (source column) and `as` (output column).
- The resulting record has exactly the listed columns, **in the listed
  order**.
- If a source column is missing from the incoming record, the output value
  for that column is the empty string `""`.
- Rename collisions (two entries produce the same output name) are a config
  error.

#### `sort`

```yaml
- type: sort
  keys:
    - column: amount
      order: desc
      as: number
    - column: customer
      order: asc
```

- `keys` is an ordered list of sort keys.
- Each key has `column` (required), `order` (`asc` or `desc`, default
  `asc`), and `as` (`string` or `number`, default `string`).
- `as: number` requires every record's value for that column to parse as a
  finite decimal number. A non-parsable value is a hard error (abort the
  run).
- Sort is **stable**: records that tie on every key preserve their input
  order.
- A missing key column in a record is treated as the empty string for
  `string` comparisons, and as a hard error for `number` comparisons.

#### `limit`

```yaml
- type: limit
  count: 10
```

- `count` is a non-negative integer. After the stage, at most `count`
  records remain, in the same order as they entered the stage. `count: 0`
  legitimately produces zero records.

An unknown stage `type` is a config error.

### `output`

```yaml
output:
  path: out.jsonl   # or "-" to write to stdout
  format: jsonl     # only "jsonl" is supported in this milestone
  columns: [order_id, customer, amount]   # optional; see below
```

- `format` must be `jsonl`. Any other value is a config error.
- `path` is resolved relative to the config directory, unless it is exactly
  `-`, which means "write to stdout".
- `jsonl` is JSON Lines: one JSON object per line terminated by `\n`, no
  trailing blank line, no leading BOM.
- If `columns` is **absent**, every record is serialised with its current
  columns in the record's own order. Records are emitted in the stage
  pipeline's output order.
- If `columns` is **present**, it is a list of strings that defines the
  **exact output schema**: every emitted line must have those keys, in that
  order. A missing column in a record is serialised as the empty string;
  any extra column present on the record is dropped for emission (but still
  participated in stages).
- Every value is emitted as a JSON string — even numeric-looking ones —
  because the pipeline treats record fields as strings. JSON escaping rules:
  backslash, double quote, and ASCII control characters below 0x20 are
  escaped (`\\`, `\"`, `\n`, `\r`, `\t`, and `\u00XX` for other control
  codes). Non-ASCII UTF-8 bytes are emitted verbatim (no `\u` escapes).
- When `path` is a file, the output file is truncated at the start of the
  run and written in full at the end; a failed run must not leave a partial
  output file behind (treat it as all-or-nothing — you may either remove
  any file you were going to write, or leave the previous file untouched).
- When `path` is `-`, records go to stdout in the same format. On a failed
  run, it is acceptable to have already written some records to stdout
  before the error — stdout is not rewindable — but the summary line (see
  below) must not be printed.

## Run summary

On a successful run, `flowctl` must print exactly one line to stderr, in this
form:

```
flowctl: processed=<N> emitted=<M> dropped_nonnumeric=<D1> dropped_missing=<D2>
```

- `N` is the number of records read from the input source.
- `M` is the number of records actually written to the output.
- `D1` is the total count of "drop non-numeric row" events that occurred
  across all filter stages this run.
- `D2` is the total count of "missing column" drops across all filter
  stages this run (count every occurrence, not only the first-reported).
- The summary line is printed only on exit code `0`.

Any other diagnostic stderr lines must be printed **before** the summary
line so the summary is always the last stderr line of a successful run. No
lines may go to stdout when `output.path` is not `-`.

Exit code is `0` on success, non-zero on any failure described above.

## Examples

Given `orders.csv`:

```
order_id,customer,amount,currency,status
1001,alice,42.50,USD,paid
1002,bob,17.00,USD,pending
1003,carol,120.00,EUR,paid
```

and `pipeline.yaml`:

```yaml
input:
  path: orders.csv
  format: csv
stages:
  - type: filter
    where:
      column: status
      op: "=="
      value: paid
  - type: sort
    keys:
      - column: amount
        order: desc
        as: number
  - type: project
    columns:
      - order_id
      - name: customer
        as: buyer
      - amount
output:
  path: out.jsonl
  format: jsonl
```

`out.jsonl` must contain exactly:

```
{"order_id":"1003","buyer":"carol","amount":"120.00"}
{"order_id":"1001","buyer":"alice","amount":"42.50"}
```

and stderr must contain exactly:

```
flowctl: processed=3 emitted=2 dropped_nonnumeric=0 dropped_missing=0
```

## What to ship

- `/app/cmd/flowctl/` (CLI entry point) and whatever internal packages you
  need under `/app/internal/`.
- `/app/go.mod` updated as needed (still no third-party dependencies).
- A working `/app/flowctl` binary produced by `go build -o /app/flowctl
  ./cmd/flowctl` from `/app/`.

Keep the existing `/app/README.md` and `/app/examples/` in place; they
describe the project context and are not part of the deliverable.

rounds.title: Add a SQLite input source and a grouping stage
rounds.md:
# Add a SQLite input source and a grouping stage

Grow `flowctl` so the pipeline can pull records out of a SQLite database and
collapse many input rows into aggregated summary rows. Keep every existing behavior exactly as it is; this change only adds
features. Nothing in the CLI invocation changes.

## New input format: `sqlite`

```yaml
input:
  path: orders.db
  format: sqlite
  query: "SELECT order_id, customer, amount, status FROM orders ORDER BY order_id"
```

- `path` is a path to a SQLite database file, resolved relative to the
  directory containing the config file.
- `query` is a required non-empty string. It is an arbitrary `SELECT`
  statement that must be executed exactly as written — `flowctl` must not
  rewrite it. The column order of the record is the column order of the
  result set (i.e. the order of the `SELECT`'s output columns).
- Missing `query` is a config error (`flowctl: config: ...`).
- Every result-set value must be convertible to a string by these rules:
  - `TEXT` -> its string value
  - `INTEGER` -> its decimal representation with no leading zeros, no
    thousands separators, and a leading `-` for negatives (e.g. `42`,
    `-7`, `0`)
  - `REAL` -> a lossless decimal rendering. Integer-valued reals like
    `3.0` are rendered as `3`; others use Go's default `%g` rendering
    (e.g. `3.14`, `1.5e-07`). Only values with an unambiguous decimal form need stable handling here;
    platform-specific `%g` edge cases for irrational values are out of scope.
  - `NULL` -> the empty string `""`
  - `BLOB` is a hard error (abort the run with `flowctl: input: ...`).
- If the SQLite file does not exist, is unreadable, or the query errors out
  at execution time, abort the run with a non-zero exit code and a single
  stderr line starting with `flowctl: input: `.
- The container has no network access. The `sqlite3` CLI is available at
  `/usr/bin/sqlite3`; you may shell out to it if you prefer, or parse the
  database file yourself — either is fine as long as the behaviour above
  is met.

Records produced by `sqlite` count toward `processed` in the summary line
exactly like CSV / NDJSON records do.

## New stage: `group`

```yaml
- type: group
  by: [status, currency]
  aggregates:
    - as: n
      op: count
    - as: total
      op: sum
      column: amount
    - as: min_amount
      op: min
      column: amount
    - as: max_amount
      op: max
      column: amount
    - as: avg_amount
      op: avg
      column: amount
```

Semantics:

- `by` is an ordered non-empty list of column names. An empty or missing
  `by` is a config error.
- Each incoming record is assigned a group key: the tuple of the record's
  values for the columns named in `by`, in that order. A record missing
  one of the `by` columns is a hard error (abort the run with
  `flowctl: group: ...`). There is no implicit "null group".
- `aggregates` is a non-empty list. Each aggregate has:
  - `as`: the output column name (required, non-empty, unique within the
    stage).
  - `op`: one of `count`, `sum`, `min`, `max`, `avg`.
  - `column`: required for every op except `count`. Missing for `count`.
- Semantics of each `op`:
  - `count` counts records in the group; its value is a non-negative
    integer rendered in the output as its decimal representation.
  - `sum`, `min`, `max`, `avg` operate on the numeric parse of
    `record[column]`. A value that does not parse as a finite decimal
    number is a hard error (abort the run with
    `flowctl: group: non-numeric value in column <col> at row <n>`, where
    `<n>` is the 1-based input record number).
  - `sum` of an empty group is `0`. `avg` of an empty group is undefined,
    but empty groups do not occur in practice (groups only exist if at
    least one record landed in them). `min` / `max` of a group with one
    or more records return the smallest / largest numeric value.
  - Output rendering: numeric aggregate values are rendered by the same
    rules as SQLite `REAL` above: integer-valued results drop the
    trailing `.0` (e.g. `42`, `-7`, `0`); others use Go `%g`.
- **Output row schema after a `group` stage**: the output record has
  exactly `len(by) + len(aggregates)` columns, in the order
  `by[0], by[1], ..., aggregates[0].as, aggregates[1].as, ...`. Any
  other columns from the incoming records are dropped at this stage.
- **Emission order**: groups are emitted in **first-seen order**. That is,
  the first record whose `by` tuple establishes a new group defines that
  group's position in the output. Groups emitted in this stage count
  toward `emitted` in the summary only after any downstream stages
  consume them.

A `group` stage is a full barrier: it consumes all records before producing
any output record. Downstream stages (including another `group`, a `sort`,
a `limit`, etc.) see the group's output rows, not the original input rows.

## Interactions with existing stages

- `filter`, `project`, `sort`, `limit` continue to work on whatever record
  stream they see. After a `group`, the record stream has exactly the
  schema the `group` stage produced — earlier columns are gone.
- The input record numbering (used for `flowctl: filter: drop non-numeric
  row <n>` and the new `flowctl: group: non-numeric value ... at row <n>`
  diagnostic) always refers to the 1-based input record number counted in
  the order the input source produced records — it is **not** reset by
  `group` or any other stage.
- Under `--strict`, a non-numeric aggregate value is already a hard error
  independent of `--strict`; `--strict` does not change `group` behaviour.
  But filter-drop behaviour under `--strict` continues exactly as in
  the existing CSV and NDJSON sources.

## Summary line

Unchanged in format:

```
flowctl: processed=<N> emitted=<M> dropped_nonnumeric=<D1> dropped_missing=<D2>
```

- `N` still counts input-source records.
- `M` still counts records actually emitted to the output (which, after a
  `group`, is the number of groups that survived any downstream filter /
  limit).
- `D1`, `D2` still count filter-stage drops only. `group`-stage aborts are
  not "drops"; they are hard errors and terminate the run before any
  summary line is printed.

## Compatibility

- Existing CSV and NDJSON pipelines continue to produce exactly the same
  output and summary line. Adding a SQLite input or a `group` stage is always opt-in
  via the YAML config — pipelines that don't use them are unchanged.
- The build command and CLI invocation are unchanged:

  ```
  go build -o /app/flowctl ./cmd/flowctl
  /app/flowctl run --config <path> [--strict]
  ```

- `go.mod` module path stays at `example.com/flowctl`. You may add one
  Go standard-library-only package internally; you still must not pull in
  any third-party Go dependency.

## Example

Given `orders.db` with table `orders(order_id INTEGER, customer TEXT, amount REAL, currency TEXT, status TEXT)` holding:

```
1001,alice,42.50,USD,paid
1002,bob,17.00,USD,pending
1003,carol,120.00,EUR,paid
1004,dave,5.00,USD,paid
1005,eve,9.00,EUR,pending
```

and `pipeline.yaml`:

```yaml
input:
  path: orders.db
  format: sqlite
  query: "SELECT order_id, customer, amount, currency, status FROM orders ORDER BY order_id"
stages:
  - type: filter
    where:
      column: status
      op: "=="
      value: paid
  - type: group
    by: [currency]
    aggregates:
      - as: n
        op: count
      - as: total
        op: sum
        column: amount
      - as: avg_amount
        op: avg
        column: amount
  - type: sort
    keys:
      - column: total
        order: desc
        as: number
output:
  path: out.jsonl
  format: jsonl
```

`out.jsonl` must contain exactly:

```
{"currency":"EUR","n":"1","total":"120","avg_amount":"120"}
{"currency":"USD","n":"2","total":"47.5","avg_amount":"23.75"}
```

and stderr (summary) must be:

```
flowctl: processed=5 emitted=2 dropped_nonnumeric=0 dropped_missing=0
```

rounds.title: Add a `join` stage so pipelines can enrich records from a second source
rounds.md:
# Add a `join` stage so pipelines can enrich records from a second source

Grow `flowctl` so that a pipeline can pull rows from a **second** data source
and splice fields from those rows onto the records flowing through the
pipeline. All existing behavior stays as-is; this change only adds a
new stage type called `join`.

## New stage: `join`

```yaml
- type: join
  kind: left            # or "inner"
  on: [customer]         # one or more columns
  right:
    input:
      path: customers.csv
      format: csv
    select: [customer, tier, region]   # optional; see below
    prefix: ""             # optional; see below
```

### Shape of the config

- `kind` is required; must be either `inner` or `left`. Anything else is a
  config error (`flowctl: config: ...`).
- `on` is a non-empty ordered list of column names. These columns must be
  present on both the **left** records (the ones flowing through the
  pipeline right now) and the **right** records (loaded as described
  below). `on` names the columns that must match for two rows to be joined.
- `right` is required and is itself a block that describes the second
  source:
  - `right.input` has exactly the same shape as the top-level `input` from
    the existing source contract: required `path` and `format`, optional
    `query` when `format: sqlite`. All three supported formats (`csv`, `ndjson`,
    `sqlite`) must be accepted. `path` is resolved relative to the
    pipeline config file's directory — identical resolution rules to the
    top-level `input`.
  - `right.select` (optional) is a list of column names to keep from the
    right records before the join. It defines the right-side schema:
    each right record is projected to **exactly** the columns listed,
    in the order listed. If a listed column is missing from a given
    right record, it becomes `""` on that record. If `right.select` is
    omitted, the right schema is taken from the **first right record's**
    column order (for CSV: the header order; for NDJSON: the key order
    as they appear in the first line; for SQLite: the `SELECT` column
    order). Subsequent right records with keys not in that first-record
    schema have those extra keys silently ignored; missing columns
    become `""`.
  - `right.prefix` (optional string, default `""`) is a string prefixed to
    every **non-`on`** right column name when it is added onto the left
    record. The `on` columns themselves keep their original name (they
    would never be renamed, and they are the same on both sides anyway).

### Semantics

- Loading the right side happens **once**, at the start of the `join`
  stage.
- **Error prefix for the right source is `flowctl: input:`**, exactly the
  same prefix as the top-level input. Any error while opening, reading,
  parsing, or decoding `right.input` (missing file, unreadable file,
  malformed CSV/NDJSON/SQLite, SQLite query error, etc.) must be emitted
  on stderr as a single line starting with `flowctl: input: ` and the
  process must exit non-zero. For example, a missing right-side CSV
  produces a stderr line starting with `flowctl: input: ` (the remainder
  of the line can mention the path and the underlying OS error). Do NOT
  emit these errors under a different prefix (e.g. `flowctl: join:` or
  `flowctl: open:`); right-source I/O errors belong to the `input:`
  error class.
- A left record is matched against right records by equality of the
  string values of the `on` columns. The match is string equality — no
  numeric coercion, no whitespace trimming, no case folding.
- **Empty-string on-column values never match.** If *any* of a left
  record's `on` column values equals `""` (the empty string), the left
  record is treated as having **no matches**, regardless of whether a
  right record also has `""` in the corresponding columns. Symmetrically,
  a right record with `""` in any `on` column is **excluded** from the
  match index when the right side is loaded — it is not an error, but
  it will never participate in a match. This means:
  - `kind: left` with a left empty-key record still emits exactly one
    output row (with `""` for every non-`on` right-side column).
  - `kind: inner` with a left empty-key record emits nothing.
  - Right records with empty-string `on` values are silently dropped
    from the right index (they are still counted by `right.input`'s
    successful load — they do not raise an error on load).
- A left record whose `on` columns do not all exist is a hard error
  (abort the run with a stderr line starting with `flowctl: join: `).
  Same thing for a right record that is loaded but lacks an `on` column:
  hard error.
- When a left record matches **multiple** right records, one output row
  is emitted per match, in the right-source's record-ingest order (i.e.
  the order rows were produced by `right.input`).
- When a left record matches **no** right record:
  - `kind: left` → emit exactly one output row. For every non-`on`
    right-side column (post-`select`, post-`prefix`), the value is `""`.
  - `kind: inner` → emit nothing (the left record is dropped).
- Column order on the output record:
  1. Every column of the incoming left record, in its existing order
     (including the `on` columns).
  2. Then, for each non-`on` column of the **right-side schema**
     (defined above — either `right.select` in its listed order, or the
     first right record's column order if `select` is omitted), in that
     fixed order, prefixed by `right.prefix` if provided. The right
     column order is fixed once at the start of the stage and does not
     vary per output row.
  - A post-prefix right column name that **collides** with a left column
    name is a config error. This is detected once, when the right-side
    schema has been resolved (i.e. after loading the first right record
    or applying `right.select`). Emit exactly
    `flowctl: config: join: column name collision: <name>` on stderr
    and abort. Collisions on the `on` columns themselves don't count
    because they are not added twice.
- Value types: still strings. No numeric coercion in the join.
- Right-side values are rendered to strings **identically** to the
  top-level input of the same format (the existing SQLite input rendering rules): SQLite INTEGER is
  rendered as a decimal string (e.g. `-3` → `"-3"`); SQLite REAL is
  rendered with no trailing zeros (e.g. `3.0` → `"3"`, `0.0` → `"0"`,
  `1.5` → `"1.5"`); SQLite NULL is rendered as `""`; SQLite TEXT is
  used verbatim; SQLite BLOB is a hard error (same `flowctl: input:`
  prefix). NDJSON and CSV on the right side follow the same rules as
  on the top-level input. You may not reimplement rendering for the
  right source — it must produce exactly the same string a top-level
  input would for the same underlying value.

### Counting for the summary line

- `processed` in the summary still counts **top-level input records**
  (left side). It does **not** count right-side records.
- `emitted` still counts what was finally written to the output. After an
  inner join that filters 3-of-5 left records, `emitted` is 2 (assuming
  no downstream stage changes that further).
- `dropped_nonnumeric` and `dropped_missing` are still driven only by
  filter stages. A left record that is dropped by an inner join because
  of no match does **not** add to either counter.

### Interactions with existing stages

- The per-input row numbering (used for `flowctl: filter: drop non-numeric
  row <n>`) still refers to the **top-level input** record number. If a
  `join` expands one left record into three output rows, any later
  `filter` stage that drops those expanded rows will report the original
  input row number for all three.
- A `join` stage can appear before or after any other stage, including
  another `join`, `group`, `sort`, `limit`, `filter`, or `project`.

## Compatibility

- Existing pipelines that do not use `join` continue to produce the same output. A pipeline that
  doesn't use the `join` stage type is unaffected.
- The build command and CLI invocation are unchanged:

  ```
  go build -o /app/flowctl ./cmd/flowctl
  /app/flowctl run --config <path> [--strict]
  ```

- `go.mod` stays at module path `example.com/flowctl`, no third-party
  dependencies.

## Example

Given `orders.csv`:

```
order_id,customer,amount
1001,alice,42.50
1002,bob,17.00
1003,carol,120.00
1004,alice,5.00
```

and `customers.csv`:

```
customer,tier,region
alice,gold,US
bob,silver,EU
```

and `pipeline.yaml`:

```yaml
input:
  path: orders.csv
  format: csv
stages:
  - type: join
    kind: left
    on: [customer]
    right:
      input:
        path: customers.csv
        format: csv
      select: [customer, tier, region]
      prefix: "c_"
output:
  path: out.jsonl
  format: jsonl
```

`out.jsonl` must contain exactly:

```
{"order_id":"1001","customer":"alice","amount":"42.50","c_tier":"gold","c_region":"US"}
{"order_id":"1002","customer":"bob","amount":"17.00","c_tier":"silver","c_region":"EU"}
{"order_id":"1003","customer":"carol","amount":"120.00","c_tier":"","c_region":""}
{"order_id":"1004","customer":"alice","amount":"5.00","c_tier":"gold","c_region":"US"}
```

and the summary line is:

```
flowctl: processed=4 emitted=4 dropped_nonnumeric=0 dropped_missing=0
```

If the pipeline's `join` is changed to `kind: inner`, carol is dropped:

```
{"order_id":"1001","customer":"alice","amount":"42.50","c_tier":"gold","c_region":"US"}
{"order_id":"1002","customer":"bob","amount":"17.00","c_tier":"silver","c_region":"EU"}
{"order_id":"1004","customer":"alice","amount":"5.00","c_tier":"gold","c_region":"US"}
```

with summary `flowctl: processed=4 emitted=3 dropped_nonnumeric=0 dropped_missing=0`.

rounds.title: Add a `dedup` stage, and correct the missing-key behaviour of `sort`
rounds.md:
# Add a `dedup` stage, and correct the missing-key behaviour of `sort`

Add a `dedup` stage, and fix the existing `sort` behavior for missing
numeric keys. All other behavior remains unchanged.

## Correction to `sort` — missing key columns no longer abort

### Previous behavior

The previous rule was: *"A missing key column in a record is treated
as the empty string for `string` comparisons, and as a hard error for
`number` comparisons."*

### New behavior

- `string`-typed key: unchanged — a missing key column is treated as
  the empty string.
- `number`-typed key: **missing key columns are no longer a hard
  error.** A record that lacks the key column is sorted as if its
  value were the special sentinel "less than every finite number".
  In concrete terms:
  - With `order: asc`, records missing the key sort **before** all
    records that do have the key.
  - With `order: desc`, records missing the key sort **after** all
    records that do have the key.
  - Stable sort still applies: records that tie on every key
    (including tying on the "missing" state) preserve their relative
    input order.
- The existing rule for non-parseable (but *present*) numeric values is
  unchanged: a non-numeric, non-empty value in a `number`-typed sort
  column is still a hard error (abort the run, stderr line starting
  with `flowctl: sort: `). **Empty string** in a `number` key is
  treated like *missing* (sentinel below all finite numbers), not as
  a parsing error.
- `processed` / `emitted` / `dropped_nonnumeric` / `dropped_missing`
  counters are **unchanged**; a sort that no longer aborts on missing
  numeric keys simply emits what it would have emitted, without
  incrementing any counter.

This correction is retroactive in the sense that any test input that
previously would have aborted a pipeline due to a missing numeric sort
key must now succeed and sort those records per the new rule.

The correction does **not** affect `filter` (which still drops
non-numeric rows under numeric operators), nor `group` aggregate numeric
parsing (which still treats non-numeric aggregate inputs as hard errors).

## New stage: `dedup`

```yaml
- type: dedup
  by: [customer, region]    # one or more columns
  keep: first               # optional; see below
  on: amount                # required only for keep: min/max
  fallback: first           # optional; only meaningful with keep: min/max
  when:                     # optional; see "Conditional dedup" below
    column: status
    op: "=="
    value: open
```

### Shape

- `by` is required: a non-empty ordered list of column names. Records
  that agree on the string values of all `by` columns are considered
  duplicates (same "group").
- `keep` is optional and defaults to `first`. Must be one of:
  - `first`: emit the first record (in ingest order) for each distinct
    group, drop subsequent ones.
  - `last`: emit the **last** record (in ingest order) for each
    distinct group, drop earlier ones.
  - `min`: within each group, emit the record with the **smallest
    finite numeric value** on the column named by `on`. See "Numeric
    tie-breaker" below.
  - `max`: within each group, emit the record with the **largest
    finite numeric value** on the column named by `on`. See "Numeric
    tie-breaker" below.
  - `unique`: emit a record **only if its group has exactly one
    record**. Groups with two or more members are entirely dropped
    (all their records). This is qualitatively different from the
    other modes: there is no survivor selection — whole groups are
    retained or dropped based on group size. Output is emitted in
    ingest order (each surviving record appears at its own ingest
    position).
  Any other value is a config error (`flowctl: config: dedup: ...`).
- `on` is **required when `keep` is `min` or `max`** and is a **single
  column name** (not a list). For `keep: first`/`last`/`unique`, `on`
  must be absent (its presence is a config error). If `keep:
  min`/`max` is used without `on`, it is a config error (`flowctl:
  config: dedup: ...`). `on` may name a column that is **also** in
  `by`; that is legal but the comparison column is still required on
  each record.
- `fallback` is optional and only meaningful when `keep` is `min` or
  `max`. It must be `first` (the default) or `last`. It controls how
  the dedup resolves two cases:
  - **Tie among finite numeric values**: if two or more records in a
    group share the optimum finite `on` value, `fallback: first`
    keeps the earliest one in ingest order; `fallback: last` keeps
    the latest one.
  - **Group of only-sentinel records**: see "Numeric tie-breaker"
    below.
  Any other value is a config error (`flowctl: config: dedup: ...`).
  Providing `fallback` when `keep` is `first`, `last`, or `unique` is
  a config error.

### Semantics — grouping and emission

- Comparison of `by` values is plain string equality (no trimming, no
  case folding, no numeric coercion), just like `join.on`. Unlike
  `join.on`, **an empty-string value in a `by` column does NOT
  suppress grouping** — records with `""` in any or all `by` columns
  still form their own group (distinct from, say, `"0"` or `" "`),
  and each such group is deduped independently. Missing `by` column
  (key absent on the record, which in NDJSON means the key is not in
  the object; in CSV means the configured column name is not in the
  header at all — per-row absence is impossible for CSV) is a hard
  error (stderr line starting with `flowctl: dedup: `, abort).
- Empty `by` list is a config error (`flowctl: config: dedup: ...`).
- Output order depends on `keep`:
  - `keep: first` → the first record for each distinct group is
    emitted **in the order those first records were seen** in the
    input stream.
  - `keep: last` → the last record for each distinct group is
    emitted in the relative order those surviving last records
    appeared in the input stream.
  - `keep: min` / `keep: max` → after picking the survivor per group,
    the survivors are emitted in the order their **ingest positions**
    appear in the input stream. (Equivalently: collect one record
    per group in the input's relative order of the surviving
    records.)
  - `keep: unique` → records belonging to size-1 groups are emitted
    in their original ingest order. Records belonging to size-≥2
    groups are dropped entirely.

### Semantics — numeric tie-breaker (only `keep: min`/`max`)

Each record in a group has a value on `on`:

- **Finite**: the `on` column is present with a non-empty string that
  parses as a finite decimal number (same parsing rules as the current
  `sort` number-typed key — leading/trailing whitespace is NOT
  stripped; sign optional; exponent allowed; infinities/NaN are NOT
  accepted and count as non-parseable).
- **Sentinel**: the `on` column is absent on the record, or present
  but the value is exactly the empty string. Treated as the special
  sentinel "less than every finite number" (same convention as the
  sort correction above).
- **Non-parseable**: the `on` column is present with a non-empty,
  non-numeric string. **Hard error** (stderr line starting with
  `flowctl: dedup: `, abort) — regardless of whether this record
  would have won or lost. The check fires as soon as a non-parseable
  `on` value is encountered, even in groups that already have a
  clear winner.

Survivor selection within a group:

1. If the group contains at least one **finite** record, the survivor
   is chosen only from the finite records. For `keep: min` the
   survivor has the smallest finite value; for `keep: max` the
   largest. Sentinel records in the same group are discarded.
2. If **all** records in the group are sentinel (everyone is
   missing/empty), every record is tied. The survivor is chosen per
   `fallback`: `first` → earliest by ingest order, `last` → latest.
   This is the *only* way a sentinel record can be emitted by
   `keep: min`/`max`.
3. If multiple finite records tie on the optimum, the survivor is
   chosen per `fallback`: `first` → earliest by ingest order, `last`
   → latest.

Observation: `keep: min` does NOT systematically prefer sentinel records
over finite ones even though sentinel is "less than every finite
number". The dedup semantics treat sentinel as a lower-priority bucket
that only wins if nothing else is present in the group. This mirrors
how real data pipelines want "give me the row with the smallest non-
null value per group, ignoring nulls unless there are only nulls". (In
contrast, `sort` places sentinel first under `asc` because sort is a
total order, not a survivor-selection.) You should not conflate the
two rules.

### Conditional dedup (`when`)

`when` is an optional predicate with the same shape as the existing
`filter.where` clause (required `column`, `op`, `value`, with the
same set of supported `op` values: `==`, `!=`, `>`, `>=`, `<`, `<=`).
Any other `op` value inside `dedup.when` is a configuration error and
must fail before processing with a stderr line starting with
`flowctl: config: dedup: `.
It subsets which records participate in deduplication:

- Records **matching** `when` are deduplicated per the rules above:
  grouped by `by`, one survivor per group picked per `keep`/`on`/
  `fallback`.
- Records **not matching** `when` (including records whose `when.column`
  is missing or whose numeric comparison fails the same way a `filter`
  numeric comparison fails, see below) pass through **unchanged**, in
  their original ingest order, and are NOT grouped or deduped. They
  do not contribute to any group's "first"/"last"/"min"/"max"
  selection even if they would have shared a `by` key with a record
  that does participate.
- Numeric operators (`>`, `>=`, `<`, `<=`) on `when` follow the existing
  `filter` rules verbatim: a record whose `when.column` is missing or
  non-numeric under a numeric `op` is **NOT** considered matching and
  simply passes through unchanged (it does not abort, it does not
  increment `dropped_nonnumeric` or `dropped_missing` — those counters
  are reserved for `filter`). The string operators `==` / `!=` compare
  string values directly; a missing column for `==` / `!=` is NOT a
  match.
- After selection, the output stream interleaves **pass-through
  records** and **dedup-survivor records** in the original input's
  relative ingest order — i.e. each output record appears at the
  position of the input record it came from (for pass-through records
  that is the record itself; for survivors it is the ingest position
  of the surviving record). This preserves the read-order of the
  input as much as possible.
- `by` column absence checks still apply, but only to records that
  match `when`. A pass-through record may lack `by` columns without
  causing a hard error. An `on`-column non-parseable value is only a
  hard error if the offending record matches `when` (pass-through
  records are not inspected for `on`).

An absent `when` means "every record participates in dedup" (the
default behaviour described earlier).

For `keep: unique` under `when`, group membership is computed only
over participating records. A non-participating record never
contributes to group size, even if its `by` values would have matched
a participating group. Pass-through records are emitted unchanged at
their original positions, independent of whether the matching
participating group they share `by` values with happens to be
size-1 or size-≥2.

### Counting for the summary line

- `processed` still counts top-level input records (pre-dedup count).
- `emitted` reflects records actually written to the output.
- `dropped_nonnumeric` and `dropped_missing` are not touched by dedup;
  a `keep: min`/`max` that encounters a non-parseable value aborts
  rather than dropping. `when`-based pass-through does NOT touch any
  counter.

### Column order

The output record's column order is identical to the input record's
column order for each surviving record (dedup does not add, remove,
or reorder columns).

### Interactions

- `dedup` can appear before or after any existing stage (`filter`,
  `project`, `sort`, `limit`, `group`, `join`, another `dedup`).
- The existing filter rule still stands for `filter`: if a later `filter`
  reports `drop non-numeric row <n>`, `<n>` is the **top-level input**
  record number of the record that survived dedup, not a post-dedup
  index.
- When `keep: min`/`max` and `on` names a column that a later stage
  then drops via `project`, the comparison has already happened — the
  survivor is picked first, then projection applies.

## Compatibility

- Existing pipelines that do NOT use `dedup` and do NOT have a
  `sort` over a `number`-typed key with missing values continue to
  produce the same output.
- Pipelines that used to abort due to missing numeric sort keys will
  now succeed and emit the sorted records per the correction above.
- The build command and CLI invocation are unchanged:

  ```
  go build -o /app/flowctl ./cmd/flowctl
  /app/flowctl run --config <path> [--strict]
  ```

- `go.mod` stays at module path `example.com/flowctl`, no third-party
  dependencies.

## Examples

### Dedup first/last example

Given `orders.csv`:

```
order_id,customer,amount
1001,alice,42.50
1002,bob,17.00
1003,alice,5.00
1004,carol,1.00
1005,bob,99.00
```

With `- type: dedup\n  by: [customer]`, `out.jsonl`:

```
{"order_id":"1001","customer":"alice","amount":"42.50"}
{"order_id":"1002","customer":"bob","amount":"17.00"}
{"order_id":"1004","customer":"carol","amount":"1.00"}
```

summary `flowctl: processed=5 emitted=3 dropped_nonnumeric=0 dropped_missing=0`.

With `keep: last`:

```
{"order_id":"1003","customer":"alice","amount":"5.00"}
{"order_id":"1004","customer":"carol","amount":"1.00"}
{"order_id":"1005","customer":"bob","amount":"99.00"}
```

(surviving records emitted in the relative order those surviving
records appeared in the input: 1003, then 1004, then 1005.)

### Dedup min/max example

With the same input and pipeline:

```yaml
stages:
  - type: dedup
    by: [customer]
    keep: min
    on: amount
```

alice's group has finite values 42.50 and 5.00 → min is 5.00 (record
1003). bob's group has finite 17.00 and 99.00 → min is 17.00 (record
1002). carol has one record, 1.00. Survivors in input-relative order
are 1002, 1003, 1004:

```
{"order_id":"1002","customer":"bob","amount":"17.00"}
{"order_id":"1003","customer":"alice","amount":"5.00"}
{"order_id":"1004","customer":"carol","amount":"1.00"}
```

With `keep: max`:

- alice: max finite 42.50 (record 1001).
- bob: max finite 99.00 (record 1005).
- carol: 1.00 (record 1004).

Survivors in input-relative order are 1001, 1004, 1005:

```
{"order_id":"1001","customer":"alice","amount":"42.50"}
{"order_id":"1004","customer":"carol","amount":"1.00"}
{"order_id":"1005","customer":"bob","amount":"99.00"}
```

### Dedup min with sentinel example

Given `runs.csv`:

```
user,duration
alice,
alice,3.0
alice,
bob,
bob,
```

(Three alice rows and two bob rows; alice has one finite `3.0` and
two empty; bob has only empties.)

With `keep: min, on: duration, fallback: first`:

- alice's group contains at least one finite (3.0), so alice's
  survivor is the finite record (alice,3.0). The two empty alice rows
  are discarded because a finite record exists in the group.
- bob's group is all-sentinel (both empty). Both bob rows tie;
  `fallback: first` picks the earliest — the first bob row.

Output contains one alice row (3.0) and one bob row (first empty).

### Sort correction example

Given `data.csv`:

```
id,score
1,10
2,
3,7
4,20
```

and pipeline:

```yaml
input:
  path: data.csv
  format: csv
stages:
  - type: sort
    keys:
      - column: score
        as: number
        order: asc
output:
  path: out.jsonl
  format: jsonl
```

Under the new rule this succeeds; the old behavior would have aborted.
`out.jsonl`:

```
{"id":"2","score":""}
{"id":"3","score":"7"}
{"id":"1","score":"10"}
{"id":"4","score":"20"}
```

with summary `flowctl: processed=4 emitted=4 dropped_nonnumeric=0 dropped_missing=0`.

With `order: desc`:

```
{"id":"4","score":"20"}
{"id":"1","score":"10"}
{"id":"3","score":"7"}
{"id":"2","score":""}
```

rounds.title: Add a `rank` stage for per-group ordered row-numbering
rounds.md:
# Add a `rank` stage for per-group ordered row-numbering

Add a `rank` stage for ordered row-numbering within each group. All
existing behavior remains unchanged.

## New stage: `rank`

```yaml
- type: rank
  partition_by: [region, customer]   # optional; defaults to []
  order_by:                          # required, non-empty
    - column: amount
      as: number
      order: desc
      null_position: last            # required per key (first|last)
    - column: order_id
      as: string
      order: asc
      null_position: last            # required per key
  method: dense                      # optional; see below
  into: rnk                          # required
  start: 1                           # optional; default 1 (not accepted for method: percent)
  skip_nulls: false                  # optional; default false
  emit_peers: peers                  # optional; write peer-count column
  reset_on: category                 # optional; restart rank on value change
  bounds:                            # optional; abort on out-of-range rank
    min: 1
    max: 100
```

### Shape

- `partition_by` is optional: an ordered list of column names (may be
  empty, in which case all records form one partition). Records that
  agree on the string values of all `partition_by` columns form one
  partition. Comparison rules follow the existing `dedup.by` behavior
  (plain string equality; empty-string value does NOT suppress
  grouping; a missing `partition_by` column on a record that
  **participates in ranking** is a hard error: stderr line starting
  with `flowctl: rank: `, abort). "Per-row absence" has the same
  NDJSON-vs-CSV meaning as in the existing per-row absence convention.
- `order_by` is required: a non-empty ordered list of sort keys.
  Each entry has `column` (string, required), `as` (`string` or
  `number`, required — no default), `order` (`asc` or `desc`,
  required — no default), and `null_position` (`first` or `last`,
  **required** — no default; any missing or other value is a
  config error `flowctl: config: rank: null_position must be first
  or last`). `null_position` overrides the existing sentinel
  placement rule for THIS stage only (the normal `sort` stage is
  unchanged): a record whose value for this key is
  "sentinel"-equivalent (missing/empty for `as: number`; empty
  string for `as: string`) is placed **first** in the sorted
  partition (ahead of every non-sentinel record) when
  `null_position: first`, and **last** (behind every non-sentinel
  record) when `null_position: last`, regardless of `order` on
  that key. Among sentinel-equivalent records, relative order on
  that key is broken by subsequent keys in `order_by` and finally
  by ingest order. Non-sentinel records continue to compare by
  `order`. Non-parseable present numeric value is a hard error
  (`flowctl: rank: `). An empty `order_by` list is a config error
  (`flowctl: config: rank: ...`).
- `method` is optional, default `dense`. Must be one of:
  - `dense`: within each partition, records tied on the full
    `order_by` tuple receive the same rank; the next distinct tuple
    gets the next integer (no gaps). Classic SQL `DENSE_RANK()`.
  - `rank`: records tied on the full `order_by` tuple share a rank;
    the next distinct tuple skips the count of ties. Classic SQL
    `RANK()` (gaps).
  - `row`: every record gets a distinct consecutive integer within
    its partition. Tied records are broken by original ingest order.
    Classic SQL `ROW_NUMBER()`.
  - `percent`: within each partition, produces a **percent rank** —
    the relative position of each record as a fixed-precision
    decimal string. Computed in two steps: first compute the
    `rank`-style integer rank `r_i` for each participating record
    (same semantics as `method: rank`, always starting at 1
    regardless of `start`). Then write
    `p_i = (r_i - 1) / max(1, n_part - 1)` where `n_part` is the
    number of participating records in that partition. Format
    `p_i` as a **decimal string with exactly 6 digits after the
    decimal point**, using half-to-even (banker's) rounding,
    written with a single leading `0` for values in `[0, 1)`, no
    exponent, no leading `+`, and no trailing-zero trimming (e.g.
    `0` → `"0.000000"`, `1` → `"1.000000"`, `1/3` → `"0.333333"`,
    `5/12` → `"0.416667"`). A partition with exactly one
    participating record produces `"0.000000"` for that record.
    When `method: percent` is used the `start` option is a config
    error (`flowctl: config: rank: percent does not accept start`).
  Any other value is a config error (`flowctl: config: rank: ...`).
- `into` is required: a non-empty string naming the **new column**
  the rank integer will be written into. The name must NOT already
  exist on any input record that participates in ranking (presence
  on such a record is a hard error: stderr line starting with
  `flowctl: rank: `, abort, even if the agent would otherwise have
  ranked the record). "Already exist" means the key is present on
  the input record, irrespective of its value (even if empty
  string). For records that do not participate in ranking (see
  `skip_nulls` below), the column is NOT written; the record passes
  through unchanged.
- `start` is optional: an integer, default 1. The rank of the first
  distinct `order_by` tuple per partition is `start`. Must be a
  non-negative integer literal (no leading `+`, no decimals, no
  exponent). Any other shape is a config error (`flowctl: config:
  rank: ...`).
- `skip_nulls` is optional, default `false`. When `true`, records
  whose **full** `order_by` tuple is "all-sentinel" (every
  `order_by` key is numeric-sentinel OR is a string-typed key whose
  value is the empty string) do NOT participate in ranking:
  - They are emitted unchanged at their original ingest positions.
  - They do NOT contribute to the tie count for `rank` method.
  - They do NOT advance the counter for `dense`/`row` methods.
  - They do NOT participate in the `into`-column-already-present
    check and do NOT receive the `into` column in output.
  - They are NOT considered for the missing-`partition_by` hard
    error (a non-participating record may lack partition columns
    without aborting).
  When `false` (default), every record participates and sentinel
  rows are ranked according to normal sentinel/order semantics
  (placement controlled by `null_position` per key).
- `reset_on` is optional: a non-empty string naming a column.
  When set, the rank counter within a partition is **reset** every
  time the value of that column changes from one record to the
  next in **sorted order** (not ingest order). Concretely: within
  each partition's sorted list of participating records, split
  the list into **runs** of consecutive records sharing the same
  `reset_on` value; each run is ranked independently starting
  from `start`, using the selected `method`, as if the run were
  its own partition for the purposes of counter state. Peer
  counts under `emit_peers` are computed within the enclosing
  partition, not within the run. Records that lack the `reset_on`
  column are treated as having an empty-string value for grouping
  purposes (i.e. contiguous absentees form one run together).
  `reset_on` is a config error when `method: percent` is in use
  (`flowctl: config: rank: reset_on not allowed with percent`),
  when the column equals `into`, or when the column equals
  `emit_peers`. An empty string for `reset_on` is a config error.
- `bounds` is optional: a mapping with keys `min` (non-negative
  integer, required when `bounds` is present) and `max`
  (non-negative integer, required when `bounds` is present; must
  be `>= min`). Any rank-integer value computed under `method` in
  {`dense`, `rank`, `row`} that falls outside `[min, max]` is a
  hard error (`flowctl: rank: rank X out of bounds [min, max]`).
  `bounds` is a config error with `method: percent` (`flowctl:
  config: rank: bounds not allowed with percent`). Both keys must
  be supplied; missing `min` or `max` is a config error.
- `emit_peers` is optional; when present it must be a non-empty
  string naming a **second new column** to append to each
  participating record. The value written is the **peer count** for
  that record within its partition, formatted as a decimal string
  with no padding (e.g. `"1"`, `"3"`). Peer count is defined as the
  number of records in the partition (excluding records excluded
  by `skip_nulls: true`) whose **full `order_by` tuple** equals
  this record's tuple — so a record tied with two others has peer
  count `3`; a uniquely-valued record has peer count `1`. Under
  `method: row`, every participating record is its own peer group
  (peer count is always `1`), since `row` ignores ties. The same
  presence rules as `into` apply: `emit_peers` must NOT already be
  a key on any participating input record (hard error `flowctl:
  rank: ...`); it may coincide with `into` only if explicitly the
  same name (this latter case is a config error `flowctl: config:
  rank: emit_peers must differ from into`). `emit_peers` is NOT
  written for non-participating records (same as `into`). An empty
  string for `emit_peers` is a config error.

### Semantics — ordering and ranking

Within each partition:

1. Take the list of participating records (all records in the
   partition if `skip_nulls: false`; only records whose `order_by`
   tuple is not all-sentinel if `skip_nulls: true`).
2. Perform a **stable** sort using `order_by`: the same total-order
   semantics as the current `sort.keys` behavior. Ties on
   the full `order_by` tuple preserve original ingest order.
3. Assign ranks per `method`:
   - `dense`: walk the sorted list; the first record gets `start`;
     each subsequent record whose `order_by` tuple equals the
     previous keeps the same rank; each record whose tuple differs
     from the previous gets `previous_rank + 1`.
   - `rank`: first record gets `start`; ties keep the same rank;
     when the tuple changes, the new rank is `start + (number of
     records seen so far including the current one) - 1`. Concretely:
     if three records tie at rank r, the next distinct tuple gets
     rank `r + 3`.
   - `row`: every participating record in the sorted list gets the
     next integer — `start`, `start+1`, `start+2`, …, with ties
     broken by ingest order (i.e. `row` ignores ties entirely).
4. Write the computed integer into the `into` column of each
   participating record (as a **decimal string**, e.g. `"1"`, `"2"`,
   `"10"`). Do not emit `-0`, leading zeros, `+`, or whitespace.
5. Leave non-participating records unchanged.

### Semantics — output order

- The output preserves **original input ingest order**, not sort
  order. The rank integer is computed using the sort from step 2
  above, but the records are emitted in the same position they
  appeared in the input stream.
- Non-participating records (under `skip_nulls: true`) are emitted
  at their original positions, unchanged, interleaved with the
  ranked records.

### Column placement

- The `into` column is **appended to the end** of each participating
  record's column list, maintaining the original relative order of
  all pre-existing columns. For NDJSON output this simply means the
  new key is written after all pre-existing keys.
- For a participating record that has fewer pre-existing columns
  than its partition peers, the `into` column is still appended to
  that record's own column list; no padding is inserted.

### Counting for the summary line

- `processed` still counts top-level input records.
- `emitted` is the count of records actually written to output
  (ranked + non-participating pass-through records).
- `dropped_nonnumeric` and `dropped_missing` are **not touched** by
  `rank`. A `rank` stage does not drop records for any reason —
  non-parseable numeric values under an `as: number` key abort with
  `flowctl: rank: `; sentinels are handled via `skip_nulls` rather
  than dropping.

### Interactions

- `rank` can appear anywhere a stage can appear (before or after
  `filter`, `project`, `sort`, `limit`, `group`, `join`, `dedup`, or
  another `rank`).
- If a later `project` drops the column named by `into`, the rank
  computation has still happened — projection applies after
  ranking.
- If a later `sort` sorts by the `into` column, the sort key is the
  string value of that column ("10" < "2" as strings unless `as:
  number` is used; numeric-typed sort over the rank column behaves
  per the current numeric parsing rules).
- Two `rank` stages in the same pipeline must use distinct `into`
  column names unless an intermediate `project` drops the first
  one. The "into column must not already exist on input" rule is
  checked against the record as it enters the `rank` stage, so a
  second `rank` after a `project drop` is legal.

### Error prefix summary

- Config-level errors: `flowctl: config: rank: ...` (invalid
  `method`, missing `order_by`, empty `order_by`, missing `into`,
  empty `into`, bad `start`, bad `skip_nulls`, missing or
  non-`first|last` `null_position` on any order_by key, `start`
  supplied with `method: percent`, empty `emit_peers`, `emit_peers
  == into`, empty `reset_on`, `reset_on == into`, `reset_on ==
  emit_peers`, `reset_on` with `method: percent`, `bounds` with
  `method: percent`, `bounds` missing `min` or `max`, `bounds.max
  < bounds.min`, unknown field).
- Runtime errors: `flowctl: rank: ...` (missing `partition_by`
  column on a participating record, non-parseable numeric
  `order_by` value on a participating record, `into` or
  `emit_peers` column already present on a participating record,
  rank value outside `bounds`).

## Compatibility

- Existing pipelines that do NOT use `rank` continue to
  produce the same output.
- Build command and CLI invocation are unchanged:

  ```
  go build -o /app/flowctl ./cmd/flowctl
  /app/flowctl run --config <path> [--strict]
  ```

- `go.mod` stays at module path `example.com/flowctl`, no
  third-party dependencies.

## Example

Given `sales.csv`:

```
region,customer,amount,order_id
NA,alice,100,A1
NA,bob,250,A2
NA,alice,250,A3
EU,carol,50,B1
EU,dave,,B2
EU,dave,75,B3
```

and pipeline:

```yaml
input:
  path: sales.csv
  format: csv
stages:
  - type: rank
    partition_by: [region]
    order_by:
      - column: amount
        as: number
        order: desc
        null_position: last
      - column: order_id
        as: string
        order: asc
        null_position: last
    method: dense
    into: rnk
output:
  path: out.jsonl
  format: jsonl
```

Walk-through:
- NA partition, sorted by amount desc then order_id asc:
  - (250, A2) → dense rank 1
  - (250, A3) → tied with previous on full tuple? No — `order_id`
    differs. So dense rank 2.
  - (100, A1) → rank 3.
- EU partition, sorted by amount desc then order_id asc:
  - (75, B3) → rank 1 (75 is finite; 50 is also finite; sentinel for
    empty B2 is below both finites under desc, so sentinel sorts last).
  - (50, B1) → rank 2.
  - (sentinel, B2) → rank 3.

Emitted in **input order** with `rnk` appended:

```
{"region":"NA","customer":"alice","amount":"100","order_id":"A1","rnk":"3"}
{"region":"NA","customer":"bob","amount":"250","order_id":"A2","rnk":"1"}
{"region":"NA","customer":"alice","amount":"250","order_id":"A3","rnk":"2"}
{"region":"EU","customer":"carol","amount":"50","order_id":"B1","rnk":"2"}
{"region":"EU","customer":"dave","amount":"","order_id":"B2","rnk":"3"}
{"region":"EU","customer":"dave","amount":"75","order_id":"B3","rnk":"1"}
```

Summary: `flowctl: processed=6 emitted=6 dropped_nonnumeric=0 dropped_missing=0`.

rounds.title: Add a `pivot` stage, and relax `group`'s missing-`by` rule
rounds.md:
# Add a `pivot` stage, and relax `group`'s missing-`by` rule

Add a `pivot` stage, and relax the existing `group` handling for
missing `by` columns. All other behavior remains unchanged.

## Relaxation to `group` — missing `by` columns are no longer a hard error

### Previous behavior

Previously: *"A record missing one of the `by` columns is a hard
error (abort the run with `flowctl: group: ...`)."*

### New behavior

- A record that lacks a `by` column is **no longer a hard error**. The
  missing value is treated as the empty string `""` for the purpose of
  forming the group key tuple — matching the way `dedup.by`
  and `rank.partition_by` already handle absent partition
  columns on participating records, except that `group`'s rule now
  NEVER aborts on missing `by` columns (where `rank.partition_by`
  still aborts, under the current `rank` behavior).
- The existing "non-parseable aggregate numeric value" rule is
  unchanged: a present-but-non-numeric value under an `op`-that-requires-
  a-number still aborts with `flowctl: group: non-numeric value in
  column <col> at row <n>`.
- A record that lacks the `column` referenced by a numeric aggregate
  (`sum`, `min`, `max`, `avg`) is treated as contributing **no value**
  to that aggregate: it is counted by `count` but skipped by numeric
  aggregates. If every record in a group lacks that column, the
  aggregate value is rendered as the empty string `""` (for `sum` this
  overrides the previous "sum of empty group is 0" rule ONLY when every
  contributing record lacked the column; if any record contributed a
  numeric value, the usual sum applies). `avg` of a group where every
  record lacks the column is `""` too; `min`/`max` likewise.
- All other existing `group` semantics (emission order, output row
  schema, barrier behaviour, row numbering for diagnostics) are
  unchanged.

Existing `group` pipelines that do not rely on missing-`by`-column
aborts continue to behave identically.

## New stage: `pivot`

```yaml
- type: pivot
  index: [region, customer]          # required, non-empty
  columns: quarter                   # required, single column
  values: amount                     # required, single column
  as: number                         # required: "string" or "number"
  agg: sum                           # required: sum|min|max|first|last|count
  fill: "0"                          # optional; default ""
  sort_columns: asc                  # optional; asc|desc|ingest; default asc
  column_prefix: q_                  # optional; default ""
  strict_schema: false               # optional; default false
  drop_empty: false                  # optional; default false
  on_conflict: error                 # optional; error|keep_first|keep_last; default error
```

### Shape

- `index` is required: a non-empty ordered list of column names that
  identify the **output row**. Comparison rules for the index tuple
  follow the current `dedup.by` behavior (plain string equality; empty-string
  value does NOT suppress grouping; a record missing an `index` column
  is treated as having an empty string for that index position — no
  abort). An empty or missing `index` is a config error
  (`flowctl: config: pivot: index must be a non-empty list`).
- `columns` is required: the name of a **single** column whose string
  values become output column names. A record missing the `columns`
  column is treated as having the empty string for that purpose;
  empty-string pivot values ARE allowed and produce an output column
  whose pivoted name (after `column_prefix` and type formatting, see
  below) comes from the empty string. An empty or missing `columns`
  setting is a config error.
- `values` is required: the name of a **single** column whose values
  will be aggregated into each cell of the pivot. An empty or missing
  `values` setting is a config error.
- `as` is required, either `string` or `number`:
  - `string`: the raw string value of `values` for each record is fed
    into the aggregator. `agg: sum|min|max|avg` are config errors
    under `as: string` (`flowctl: config: pivot: agg <x> requires as:
    number`).
  - `number`: the value of `values` is parsed per the current numeric
    parsing rules. A non-parseable present value is a
    hard error (`flowctl: pivot: non-numeric value in column <col> at
    row <n>`). A missing value (absent `values` key on the record) is
    treated as **no contribution**: the record is still counted by
    `agg: count` but contributes nothing to numeric aggregates — this
    matches the relaxed `group`-stage rule above. Empty-string value
    on a CSV record is also treated as **no contribution**.
- `agg` is required, one of:
  - `sum`: numeric only — sum of contributing values, rendered per the
    existing group numeric-rendering rules (integer values drop `.0`,
    otherwise Go `%g`).
  - `min`, `max`: numeric only — smallest/largest contributing value.
  - `avg`: numeric only — arithmetic mean of contributing values; a
    cell whose contributors all lacked the value becomes `fill`.
  - `count`: integer count of contributing records (including records
    whose `values` key is missing or empty-string, because `count`
    counts records not values); allowed under both `as: string` and
    `as: number`. Rendered as a plain decimal integer string (`"0"`,
    `"1"`, `"7"`, no leading zeros, no sign).
  - `first`, `last`: string only under `as: string` (config error
    under `as: number`: `flowctl: config: pivot: agg <x> requires as:
    string`). `first` keeps the **first-seen** value for that (index,
    column) cell in ingest order; `last` keeps the last-seen. Missing
    or empty-string `values` DO count for `first`/`last` (the
    empty-string or absent value is the contributed value, and — like
    for numeric aggregators — an absent value produces no contribution
    only for the purposes of deciding whether a cell is "empty"; for
    `first`/`last`, absent is treated as an empty string that IS a
    value).
  - Any other `agg` is a config error.
- `fill` is optional, default `""`. The string written into a cell
  that has **zero contributors** (i.e. no record contributed for that
  (index, column) combination — including no record at all landing in
  the cell). `fill` is written as-is with no further formatting.
- `sort_columns` is optional, default `asc`:
  - `asc`: output columns are sorted by their underlying pivot value
    (the raw string from the `columns` column on input, BEFORE
    `column_prefix` is applied) using plain byte-wise string
    comparison.
  - `desc`: reverse of `asc`.
  - `ingest`: output columns appear in the order the first record
    establishing that pivot value was ingested.
  - Any other value is a config error.
- `column_prefix` is optional, default `""`. A literal string prefixed
  to every pivoted output column name. The final column name is
  `column_prefix + raw_pivot_value` (no escaping, no normalisation).
  Empty prefix is allowed and equivalent to omitting the key.
- `strict_schema` is optional, default `false`:
  - `false`: if an output row has no contributing record for a
    particular pivoted column that other rows have, that cell takes
    `fill`.
  - `true`: every output row must have had at least one contributing
    record for every pivoted column that appears in the output. If
    any cell would be `fill`-because-no-contributors, abort with
    `flowctl: pivot: row <idx-tuple> missing pivot value <col>`,
    where `<idx-tuple>` is the pipe-joined index tuple (e.g.
    `NA|alice`) and `<col>` is the **raw** pivot value (no prefix).
- `drop_empty` is optional, default `false`:
  - `false`: every distinct `index` tuple seen in the input produces
    an output row.
  - `true`: an index tuple whose **every** pivoted cell ended up as
    `fill` (zero contributors across all pivot values, so the entire
    row would be `fill`-padding) is dropped from the output. This
    cannot happen under default `agg: count` (count always contributes
    because every record counts); it is meaningful mainly for numeric
    aggregators under `as: number` when every record for that index
    tuple lacked the `values` column.
- `on_conflict` is optional, default `error`. It governs only
  `agg: first` and `agg: last` (config error otherwise — `flowctl:
  config: pivot: on_conflict only valid with agg: first or agg:
  last`):
  - `error`: if more than one record would contribute to the same
    (index, column) cell under `agg: first`, abort with `flowctl:
    pivot: duplicate contributor for cell <idx-tuple>/<col>`.
    Analogous for `agg: last`.
  - `keep_first` / `keep_last`: accept multiple contributors; the
    cell's value is the first/last contributor in ingest order,
    independent of the `agg` name. Under `agg: first` the only
    `on_conflict` values that make semantic sense are `error` and
    `keep_first`; `keep_last` with `agg: first` is a config error.
    Symmetrically, `keep_first` with `agg: last` is a config error.
  - Any other value is a config error.

### Semantics — row construction

1. **Scan**: iterate the input records in ingest order. For each
   record, form the index tuple (empty string for any missing index
   column), the column key (empty string for missing `columns` key),
   and extract the `values` cell (missing / empty-string handled as
   per `as` above).
2. **Register output row**: the first time a new index tuple is seen,
   that tuple defines a new output row; the row's position in the
   output reflects the order index tuples were first seen.
3. **Register output column**: the first time a new raw pivot value
   is seen (from the `columns` column on any record), that value
   defines a new pivoted output column. Column ordering is determined
   by `sort_columns`.
4. **Accumulate**: for each (index tuple, raw pivot value) cell,
   accumulate the record's contribution per `agg`.
5. **Finalize**: after all input is consumed, build each output row
   with exactly `len(index) + (number of pivoted columns)` fields:
   first the index values in `index`-declared order, then the
   pivoted cells in the order determined by `sort_columns`.
   Column names for the pivoted cells are
   `column_prefix + raw_pivot_value`.
6. **Drop empty rows** per `drop_empty`.
7. **Strict-schema check** per `strict_schema` (on rows not dropped).

### Semantics — output format

- `pivot` is a **full barrier**, same as `group`: it consumes all
  records before producing any output record.
- Output records, when serialized as CSV, use the column order
  described above; header row names are the declared `index` column
  names followed by the pivoted column names.
- Output records, when serialized as NDJSON, are JSON objects with
  those same keys in the same order.
- Downstream stages (including another `pivot`, `sort`, `filter`,
  `project`, `limit`, `group`, `join`, `dedup`, `rank`) see only
  the pivot's output rows; the original incoming columns other than
  those in `index`, `columns`, and `values` are gone.

### Counting for the summary line

- `processed` still counts top-level input records (records scanned
  during step 1 above).
- `emitted` counts output rows actually written (after `drop_empty`
  and any downstream stages).
- `dropped_nonnumeric` and `dropped_missing` are **not touched** by
  `pivot`. A `pivot` stage does not drop records for those reasons.
  A non-parseable numeric value under `as: number` aborts with
  `flowctl: pivot: non-numeric value in column <col> at row <n>` and
  no summary line is printed.

### Interactions

- `pivot` can appear anywhere a stage can appear. A `rank` stage
  followed by a `pivot` is allowed; the `rank`-written `into` column
  can be used as `values` (under `as: string` or `as: number`) or as
  `columns` in the `pivot`.
- A later `sort`, `filter`, `project`, or `limit` operates on the
  pivoted output schema.
- Two `pivot` stages in sequence are legal; the second sees the
  first's output schema.

### Error prefix summary

- Config-level errors: `flowctl: config: pivot: ...` (missing/empty
  `index` / `columns` / `values` / `as` / `agg`; unknown `agg`;
  `agg` incompatible with `as`; unknown `sort_columns`;
  `on_conflict` with incompatible `agg`; unknown `on_conflict`;
  `strict_schema` / `drop_empty` not a boolean; unknown field).
- Runtime errors: `flowctl: pivot: ...` (non-numeric value in
  `values` under `as: number`; duplicate contributor under
  `on_conflict: error` with `agg: first|last`; strict-schema
  violation).

## Compatibility

- Existing pipelines that do NOT use `pivot` and do NOT rely
  on `group` aborting on missing-`by`-column records continue to
  produce the same output.
- Pipelines that PREVIOUSLY depended on `group` aborting on missing
  `by` columns now silently treat those records as contributing to
  an empty-string bucket. This is the intended relaxation.
- Build command and CLI invocation are unchanged:

  ```
  go build -o /app/flowctl ./cmd/flowctl
  /app/flowctl run --config <path> [--strict]
  ```

- `go.mod` stays at module path `example.com/flowctl`, no
  third-party dependencies.

## Example

Given `sales.csv`:

```
region,customer,quarter,amount
NA,alice,Q1,100
NA,alice,Q2,200
NA,bob,Q1,50
EU,carol,Q1,75
EU,carol,Q2,25
NA,alice,Q3,
```

and pipeline:

```yaml
input:
  path: sales.csv
  format: csv
stages:
  - type: pivot
    index: [region, customer]
    columns: quarter
    values: amount
    as: number
    agg: sum
    fill: "0"
    sort_columns: asc
    column_prefix: q_
output:
  path: out.csv
  format: csv
```

Walk-through:

- Three index tuples seen in order: `(NA,alice)`, `(NA,bob)`,
  `(EU,carol)`.
- Three pivot columns seen: `Q1`, `Q2`, `Q3` (asc → `Q1`, `Q2`,
  `Q3`; prefixed to `q_Q1`, `q_Q2`, `q_Q3`).
- Contributions:
  - `(NA,alice,Q1)` = 100, `(NA,alice,Q2)` = 200, `(NA,alice,Q3)`
    has an empty value → no contribution → cell = `fill` = `"0"`.
  - `(NA,bob,Q1)` = 50; Q2, Q3 have no contributors → `"0"`.
  - `(EU,carol,Q1)` = 75, `(EU,carol,Q2)` = 25; Q3 → `"0"`.

Output CSV:

```
region,customer,q_Q1,q_Q2,q_Q3
NA,alice,100,200,0
NA,bob,50,0,0
EU,carol,75,25,0
```

Summary: `flowctl: processed=6 emitted=3 dropped_nonnumeric=0 dropped_missing=0`.

rounds.title: Add an `unpivot` stage (wide-to-long reshape)
rounds.md:
# Add an `unpivot` stage (wide-to-long reshape)

Add an `unpivot` stage for wide-to-long reshaping. All existing
behavior remains unchanged.

## New stage: `unpivot`

```yaml
- type: unpivot
  id: [region, customer]               # required, non-empty
  measures: [q_Q1, q_Q2, q_Q3]         # mutually exclusive with measures_match
  measures_match:                      # mutually exclusive with measures
    prefix: q_                         # zero-or-more selectors below
    suffix: _amt
    regex: '^q_(Q[0-9]+)_(usd|eur)$'
  exclude: [bookkeeping_col]           # optional; only valid with measures_match
  variable: quarter                    # required UNLESS `axes` is supplied
  axes:                                # optional; mutually exclusive with variable
    - {name: period,   group: 1}       # each axis: name + regex capture group index
    - {name: currency, group: 2}
  value: amount                        # required, non-empty
  variable_strip_prefix: q_            # optional; default "" (ignored when axes is used)
  variable_strip_suffix: _amt          # optional; default "" (ignored when axes is used)
  drop_empty: false                    # optional; default false
  on_missing: keep_empty               # optional; keep_empty|skip|error; default keep_empty
  sort: input                          # optional; input|measure; default input
  value_type: number                   # optional; pass|number|string; default pass
```

The intent: take a record that has many "wide" measure columns (one
column per period or per metric) and emit one row **per measure** for
each input record, so a downstream stage can `group`, `sort`, or
`pivot` over the long form.

### Shape

- `id` is required: a non-empty ordered list of column names whose
  values are copied verbatim onto every emitted long-form record.
  Comparison rules and per-record-absence handling follow `dedup.by`
  from the existing `dedup.by` behavior (a record missing an `id` column is treated as having
  the empty string for that column; no abort). An empty or missing
  `id` is a config error (`flowctl: config: unpivot: id must be a
  non-empty list`). The `id` column names must be unique within the
  list (`flowctl: config: unpivot: id has duplicate column <name>`).

- Exactly one of `measures` and `measures_match` must be supplied.
  Supplying both, or neither, is a config error
  (`flowctl: config: unpivot: exactly one of measures or
  measures_match is required`).

  - `measures` is a non-empty ordered list of column names. The
    output preserves this declared order when `sort: measure` (see
    below) is in effect; for `sort: input` the per-record emission
    order is the order in which those columns appear on the
    incoming record (see "Per-record emission order" below). An
    empty `measures` list is a config error
    (`flowctl: config: unpivot: measures must be a non-empty
    list`). Duplicate names in `measures` are a config error
    (`flowctl: config: unpivot: measures has duplicate column
    <name>`). A name that also appears in `id` is a config error
    (`flowctl: config: unpivot: column <name> appears in both id
    and measures`).

  - `measures_match` is a mapping with three optional selectors —
    `prefix` (string), `suffix` (string), `regex` (Go RE2 string).
    At least one selector must be supplied. Empty-string selectors
    are config errors (`flowctl: config: unpivot: measures_match.<sel>
    must be non-empty`). Selectors compose with logical AND: a
    candidate column must satisfy every supplied selector to be
    treated as a measure. The candidate set is the input record's
    full key set, minus `id` columns and minus any `exclude`
    columns. A column whose name appears in `id` is never a measure
    even if it would match. The matched-measure list for **each
    record** is computed from that record's own keys (NDJSON-style
    inputs may differ across records); for CSV inputs the column
    set is constant per file so the matched-measure list is fixed
    once per stage execution. An invalid `regex` is a config error
    (`flowctl: config: unpivot: measures_match.regex invalid: <go
    error>`).

- `exclude` is optional, default `[]`. A list of column names to
  remove from the candidate set under `measures_match`. Has no
  effect under `measures`; supplying `exclude` together with
  `measures` is a config error (`flowctl: config: unpivot: exclude
  is only valid with measures_match`). Names that do not appear on
  any record are silently ignored.

- `variable` is **required unless `axes` is supplied** (see below).
  When supplied: the **new** column name into which the measure
  column's name is written on each emitted long-form record.
  Must be non-empty and must not collide with any `id` column or
  with `value` (`flowctl: config: unpivot: variable must differ from
  id and value`). It MAY collide with the name of an incoming
  measure column — that incoming column has been "unpivoted away"
  and will not appear on the output. Supplying both `variable` and
  `axes` is a config error (`flowctl: config: unpivot: variable and
  axes are mutually exclusive`). Supplying neither is a config error
  (`flowctl: config: unpivot: one of variable or axes is required`).

- `axes` is optional, mutually exclusive with `variable`. When
  supplied: a non-empty ordered list of axis specs that replace the
  single `variable` column with **multiple** named columns, each
  derived from a capture group of `measures_match.regex`. Each
  axis entry is a YAML mapping; the canonical syntax is flow-style
  (`{name: period, group: 1}`) but block-style
  (`- name: period\n      group: 1`) is equivalent. The existing
  YAML parser must accept both forms. Each
  axis spec is a mapping with two required keys:
  - `name`: the output column name. Must be non-empty, must not
    collide with any `id` column, must not equal `value`, and must
    be unique across axes (`flowctl: config: unpivot: axes has
    duplicate name <name>`; `flowctl: config: unpivot: axis name
    <name> must differ from id and value`).
  - `group`: a positive integer (>= 1), the 1-based capture group
    index in `measures_match.regex` whose text becomes this axis's
    cell value. Group `0` is not allowed (that would be the full
    match — use `variable` instead). A non-positive or zero group is
